---
title: "Module 7: Some Tips"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## What We've Done

- AI + code tool
  - financial analysis (mean-variance, dcf*)
  - deliverables (figures, excel, word, powerpoint)
  - small web apps (artifacts, pyodide)
- Skills (reusable instructions)

## Where We're Going

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Some Tips (Today)**]{.card-title}

- Use AI to critique AI
- Enhance AI memory
- Boris Cherny's tips
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Web Scraping with AI**]{.card-title}

- Scraping SEC EDGAR, news, transcripts
- Transcribing earnings call audio
- Static vs. dynamic pages
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Build & Deploy Agents**]{.card-title}

- The API: talking to LLMs from code
- Chatbots and web apps
- FastAPI and deployment
:::
:::

## Where We're Going (cont.)

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Corporate Deployment**]{.card-title}

- Data security: on-prem or cloud
- Guardrails and governance
- ERP and the SaaSpocalypse
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Retrieval Augmented Generation**]{.card-title}

- How RAG works
- Example: RAG for security analysis and valuation*
- Chunking, embedding, and vector search
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**AI Sentiment Analysis**]{.card-title}

- LLMs as sentiment classifiers
- News and earnings call analysis
- Trading signals from text
:::
:::

## Using AI to Evaluate AI {.section-divider}

## Four Approaches

AI output looks polished, but polished is not the same as correct. Use AI itself to [stress-test]{.amber} AI work.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Single Reviewer**]{.card-title}

- **Use a different model** --- paste the output into a competing LLM and ask it to find errors
- **Use a subagent** --- start a second conversation with the same model and ask it to critique the first
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Multiple Reviewers**]{.card-title}

- **Use multiple subagents** --- spawn parallel reviewers with different lenses (correctness, clarity, devil's advocate)
- **Evaluate in a loop** --- have the agent produce, critique, revise, and re-critique iteratively until stable
:::
:::

[The more consequential the decision, the more reviewers you want.]{.amber}

## The /critique Skill

The `/critique` skill automates the multi-reviewer approach. It spawns [three parallel agents]{.amber} --- correctness, clarity, and devil's advocate --- then synthesizes and prioritizes the findings.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Example**

We asked Claude to research and write a 5-page Word doc on the history of the World Wide Web. Then we ran `/critique` on it.

The critique found [7 critical issues]{.amber}, 8 warnings, and 7 suggestions --- including wrong dates, understated statistics, and missing context.
::::

::::{.column}
**Try it yourself**

- [History-of-the-World-Wide-Web.docx](/files/History-of-the-World-Wide-Web.docx){target="_blank"} --- the AI-generated document
- [Critique (markdown)](/files/History-of-the-World-Wide-Web-critique.md){target="_blank"} --- the full critique report
- [Critique skill](/files/critique-skill.zip){target="_blank"} --- install this skill in Claude Code
::::
:::

## Enhance AI Memory {.section-divider}

## Saving Conversation Summaries

AI conversations are [stateless]{.amber} --- each new session starts from scratch. You can fix this by saving summaries to [markdown (.md) files]{.amber} that AI reads at startup.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**How it works**

1. At the end of a session, ask Claude to summarize what was done
2. The summary is saved as a `.md` file
3. Next session, Claude reads these files automatically
4. Continuity across days and weeks --- no need to re-explain
::::

::::{.column}
**Types of memory files**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project-level instructions loaded every session
- **Skills (.md)** --- reusable task-specific instructions
- **Session summaries** --- what happened last time
- **Memory files** --- stored observations about you and your preferences
::::
:::

[Memory turns a capable tool into a capable *collaborator*.]{.amber}

## Resource: Boris Cherny's Claude Code Tips

[Boris Cherny](https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/){target="_blank"} is the **creator and Head of Claude Code** at Anthropic. He compiled [72 tips]{.amber} on how he uses Claude Code daily, organized into 10 parts:

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
- Core workflows & parallelism
- CLAUDE.md, hooks, and verification loops
- Worktrees and subagents
- Slash commands and code quality
- Automation and scheduling
::::

::::{.column}
- Configuration and customization
- Remote control and voice mode
- Skills guide (9 skill types)
- Mobile and multi-device workflows
- Advanced: auto mode, safety classifiers
::::
:::

His tips can be **installed as a Claude Code skill** --- paste the URL in Claude Code and ask it to create a skill from the content.

## What Is Markdown?

Markdown is a plain-text format that uses simple symbols for formatting. AI reads and writes it natively.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Syntax**

```markdown
# Heading
## Subheading

- Bullet point
- **Bold text**
- *Italic text*

1. Numbered list
2. Second item

> Blockquote

`inline code`
```
::::

::::{.column}
**Where you'll see it**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project instructions
- **SKILL.md** --- skill definitions
- **Session summaries** --- conversation logs
- **README.md** --- any GitHub repo

Open any `.md` file in VS Code for a live preview, or paste it into [markdownlivepreview.com](https://markdownlivepreview.com){target="_blank"} to see it rendered.
::::
:::
